rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 4 of 4) However, as to RFP No. 4, the Court is satisfied and finds that Nonmovant has made a sufficient showing that the documents and communications exchanged with attorney Mary Youssi are protected from disclosure by the attorney-client privilege. Nonmovant has demonstrated that Mary Youssi is an attorney based in Wisconsin who Nonmovant paid with her own personal funds to assist with discovery in this matter, and that she was not consulted about trust administration matters. (L. Harris Decl., para. 13, and Declaration of Petitioner Anna Kauffman in Support of Opposition to Trustor Ilse Lemke's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, and Monetary Sanctions, para. 8.) As such, the Motion is DENIED in part as to Movant's Request for Production of Documents No. 4. The Motion is GRANTED in part as to Movant's Requests for Production of Documents Nos. 1-3, and 6. Petitioner Anna Kauffman shall provide further responses to Trustor Ilse Lemke's Request for Production of Documents, Set One, Nos. 1-3 and 6, in compliance with Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2031.220, and consistent with this ruling, within fourteen (14) days from the issuance of this Order. Movants request for sanctions pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2031.310 is DENIED. Pursuant to Section 2031.310, subd. (h), a party or attorney who unsuccessfully opposes a motion to compel further responses is subjection to monetary sanctions, unless the court finds that the one subject to sanctions acted with substantial justification, or that other circumstances make the imposition of sanctions unjust. This Court finds Nonmovant's unsuccessful opposition to the instant Motion to be substantially justified. As such, Movant's request for monetary sanctions is DENIED. The prevailing party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 3 of 4) This Court also disagrees with Nonmovant's interpretation of Moeller to the extent she believes it to hold that a successor trustee is only entitled to documents and communications related to trust administration conducted by the predecessor trustee, and not those records pertaining to the predecessor trustee's personal liability. Indeed, to the extent the Moeller court addressed this issue, it advised that a predecessor trustee "may be able to avoid disclosing [to the successor trustee the legal advice it sought in its personal capacity out of a genuine concern for possible future charges of breach of fiduciary duty] by hiring a separate lawyer and paying for legal advice out of personal funds." (supra, 16 Cal.4th at p. 1134.) Although Nonmovant hired counsel, it appears she did not begin paying for legal advice that pertained to her individual exposure to liability out of her personal funds until her removal as Co-Trustee in February of 2024. (Declaration of Lily Harris in Support of Petitioner, Anna Kauffman's Opposition to Trustor Ilse Lemke's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, and Monetary Sanctions ["L. Harris Decl."], para. 10.) As such, is appears Nonmovant did not avail herself of the ability to avoid disclosing to the current Co-Trustees the legal advice she sought related to her individual liability until February of 2024. Nonmovant also underscores Movant's dual status as both the Co-Trustee and beneficiary of the Trust and relies on Wells Fargo Bank v. Superior Court (2000) 22 Cal.4th 201 ["Wells Fargo"], for the undisputed proposition that a beneficiary is generally not entitled to a trustee's privileged communications with her attorneys. (Opposition at p. 3:6-9.) Although this Court acknowledges that Movant is indeed a beneficiary of the Trust, it does not interpret Wells Fargo to negate Movant's status as Co-Trustee such that she is precluded from asserting and waiving the attorney-client privilege. Nor does the Court interpret Wells Fargo to hold that Movant's status as a beneficiary of the Trust negates her status as Trustor and Co-Trustee such that she is barred from asserting and waiving the attorney-client privilege formerly held by Nonmovant as predecessor trustee. To be clear, Movant holds three crucial roles in the Trust: Trustor, Co-Trustee, and Beneficiary. Her status as Trustor and Co-Trustee render her differently situated from the beneficiaries in Wells Fargo. In sum, this Court does not recognize Petitioner Anna Kauffman to be the holder of the attorney-client privilege for documents and communications related to her service as former Co-Trustee of the Trust between her appointment on August 17, 2020, and her removal on February 7, 2024. In addition, Anna Kauffman has presented no evidence that she retained counsel to receive advice about her personal liability and paid counsel with her personal funds, consistent with Wells Fargo Bank v. Superior Court (2000) 22 Cal.4th 201, such that she may avoid disclosure of privileged documents and communications related to her individual liability.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 2 of 4) To establish good cause, the proponent must identify a disputed fact that is of consequence in the action and explain how the discovery sought will tend in reason to prove or disprove that fact or lead to other evidence. (Digital Music News LLC v. Superior Court (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 216, 224.) Nonmovant's underlying petition seeks a determination that Movant is unable to manage her finances and resist undue influence, to remove Movant as Co-Trustee of her Trust as a result, and to invalidate actions taken by Movant as Co-Trustee in the interim. Movant's objection to the underlying petition asserts that Nonmovant's efforts are made in bad faith. As such, this Court finds the documents and communications related to Nonmovant's service as former Co-Trustee of the Trust germane to this action. Nonmovant frames the issue as a question of whether a trustee who contests the validity of her removal must turn over attorney-client communications to a co-trustee and/or beneficiary. (Opposition at p. 2:17-21.) Although the Third Amendment removed and replaced Nonmovant as Co-Trustee of the Trust, Nonmovant's underlying petition questions Movant's capacity to manage her finances and resist undue influence, and by extension challenges the validity of Nonmovant's removal as Co-Trustee contained therein. This Court rejects Nonmovant's limited framing of the issue because it fails to acknowledge or accept the presumption of capacity enjoyed by Movant under Probate Code sec. 810. In Section 810, the Legislature established a rebuttable presumption that all persons have the capacity to make decisions and be responsible for their acts or decisions. To accept Nonmovant's position, at this juncture, would require the Court to presuppose her success on the merits of her underlying claims. Because this Court has not yet adjudicated and declared that Movant lacks capacity, Nonmovant has not yet overcome the presumption in Section 810 to invalidate her removal as Co-Trustee. For this reason, this Court declines to preclude Movant-who is one of the current Co-Trustees--from accessing and reviewing documents prepared for, and communications had with, the former Co-Trustee, Nonmovant. Nonmovant relies on Moeller v. Superior Court (1997), 16 Cal.4th 1124 ("Moeller"), for the proposition that only a successor fiduciary inherits the power to assert the attorney-client privilege, and only as to communications regarding trust administration. (Opposition at p. 8:10 - p. 11:13.) Nonmovant appears to contend that Moeller distinguishes between a successor fiduciary, and a concurrent co-fiduciary that endures the removal and replacement of its counterpart, in identifying the holder of the attorney-client privilege. This Court disagrees with Nonmovant's strained interpretation of the Moeller holding. In Moeller, the Supreme Court of California established that the attorney-client privilege vests in the office of the trustee and could not be invoked by an individual predecessor trustee against her successor(s). (supra, 16 Cal.4th at p. 1130.) At this stage in the proceedings here, consistent with the presumption provided by Probate Code section 810, this Court recognizes Movant to be a current member of the office of trustee of her Trust, and thus one holder of the attorney-client privilege Nonmovant seeks to invoke against her. This Court does not recognize Nonmovant, as former Co-Trustee, to be the rightful holder of the attorney-client privilege she seeks to invoke against Movant.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: Trustor Ilse Lemkes Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: (part 1 of 4) This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Trustor and Co-Trustee Ilse Lemke's Motion for Order to Compel Further Responses to Request for Production of Documents, Set One, and Monetary Sanctions (hereafter the "Motion"). The Motion is GRANTED in part for the reasons discussed below. Trustor and Co-Trustee Ilse Lemke ("Movant") created the Ilse Lemke Trust on June 17, 1991 (the "Trust"). Movant executed the First Amendment to the Trust on August 1, 1995. Movant executed the Second Amendment to the Trust on August 17, 2020. In the Second Amendment, Movant appointed Anna Kauffman ("Nonmovant") to serve as Co-Trustee of the Trust. On July 12, 2023, Nonmovant initiated the instant trust proceeding through the filing of her Petition for Order Settling Accounts, Passing Upon Acts of and Instructing Co-Trustee. Movant executed the Third Amendment to the Trust on February 7, 2024. (Declaration of Matthew Harris in Support of Trust Ilse Lemke's Motion to Compel Further Reponses to Request for Production of Documents, Set One, and Monetary Sanctions ["M. Harris Decl."], para. 3). The Third Amendment removed Nonmovant as Co-Trustee of the Trust and replaced her with Attorney Karen Riemer. (Ibid.) On July 9, 2024, Movant propounded Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, on Nonmovant. Nonmovant served her responses on August 13, 2024. After exhausting meet and confer efforts, Movant filed the instant Motion seeking further responses to Requests for Production of Documents ("RFP") Nos. 1-4 and 6 (collectively, "Movant's Requests"). Those requests seek documents and communications between Nonmovant and her attorneys, related to her role as former Co-Trustee of Trust. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2031.310, subd. (a)(3), the propounding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems an objection in the response to be without merit or too general. At issue here is Nonmovant's objections in response to Movant's Requests based on her assertion of the attorney-client privilege and work product doctrine. With respect to responsive documents covered by the work product doctrine, Movant's Separate Statement of Items in Dispute in Support of Trustor Ilse Lemke's Motion for Order to Compel Further Responses to Request for Production of Documents, Set One, and Monetary Sanctions (the "Separate Statement") clarifies that she is not seeking such documents in her requests. As such, the Court focuses its analysis of the Motion on the attorney-client privilege. As a preliminary matter, in opposition to the Motion, Nonmovant contends that Movant failed to comply with Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2031.310, subd. (b)(1), which required Movant to set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the discovery ought by the demands. (Petitioner, Anna Kauffman's Opposition to Trustor Ilse Lemke's Moton to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, and Monetary Sanctions [the "Opposition"], at p. 6:24 - p. 7:15.) This Court disagrees and finds that Movant established good cause.
